author: @joeglory425
text: I have a suggestion. Why not turn the hand warmer into a poorly performing battery? What I mean by this is typically it is desirable to design a battery such that it will hold a charge for a long time once it has been charged. I'm suggesting turning that idea around and designing a battery that will self-discharge fairly quickly once it has been charged up. This could be done by using a separator that deliberately has a fairly high electrical conductivity. It can't be too high or the battery will never charge up. It will just get hotter and hotter until it fails. If the conductivity is too low then the amount of heat generated won't be worthwhile so it might take a bit of experimentation to get it just right. Some other characteristics of a successful chargeable hand warmer include fast charging. Because it begins to immediately discharge once charged up that would require it to be charged immediately prior to use. No one will want to wait long for it to charge. Other characteristics would be electrodes, electrolyte, separator, and packaging that have fairly high heat tolerance. Also the battery needs pretty high energy density. Good luck!

author: @jamest.5001
text: A heated vest, gloves, and chaps, socks, it would need to be very cold for me to need all the heat, but a 23v pwm controller, or just a DC to DC with cc cv, would work nice, the 24v would make it heat faster, then drop to around 3v with the DC to DC converter, if it was just 100°F would be fine with me, maybe make gloves, with aluminized mylar reflecting the heat back to the body of the person wearing with a cotton cloth insulation, a vest with similar construction, like a light weight jacket insulation, the Battery and controller could be incorporated in to the vest, also it could be Velcro attached in to any jacket, maybe even sweatshirt, and possibly power gloves with the same power source, a couple 2200 mah cellphone sized batteries would work nicely, although a larger surface more resistance, may need more voltage.  I'm guessing multiple channel controllers, for vest chaps gloves and socks! For artic fishermen and explorers,  oil well workers, hunters, or just cold ppl living up north, or way south!! To prevent getting your pants sued  off, a few safeties is a must , if selling such a device!!

author: @kom2falcon
text: Nice job young man.  Or Dr. Smith if you've achieved reverse aging.  So my interest in this would be as a way to heat up EV Li ion batteries for charging and longer range in colder conditions.  How would the higher voltage and amps of the common 12 volt system in most autos effect this setup.  Tesla uses a rather complex liquid heating and cooling system but lower end EV mostly just use air cooling and only the internal heat of charging and use which is reduces range and increases charge time in the cold weather.  Thank you as always for your reply.

replies.author: @cayrex
replies.text: Tesla uses 350 to 375 volts,.... so how will effect the 12V setup,.... overburn, damage, etc,.... You need the right resistance of the heating material for each power type.

replies.author: @kom2falcon
replies.text:
@cayrex Not what I meant.  He is using 9 volts and I think 3 amps in the video.  Most car and motorcycles use a 12 volt and I think 5-7 amp aux port.  Tesla's use a lead acid battery to power the non car motor features that is recharged by a DC-DC converter that steps the voltage down for the lead acid battery.
It should be worth while to warm up the battery cells with a bit of resistive heating to get an over all gain in range and charge speed.
